content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance                Authorization • •    Executive/Legal  • •    Enforcement  • •    Statements of Economic Interest
(914) 322-5662                           322-5660              322-5901              322-6441                      322-6444

April 19, 1983


Jarlath Oley
Deputy General Counsel
Metropolitan Water District
of Southern California
P.O. Box 54153
Los Angeles, CA  90054

                                        Re: Your Letter of March 18, 1983,
                                            regarding FPPC Bulletin of
                                            February 7, 1983.

Dear Mr. Oley:

     You have written expressing concern over an item which
appeared in the Commission's Bulletin of February 7, 1983.
The item discussed the implications and impact of recent
legislation, AB 1040, Chap. 1049, Stats. 1982.  As you have
pointed out, the item specifically made reference to the
Metropolitan Water District ("MWD") as being covered by the
legislation.  Government Code Section 84308.1/

     You have suggested that MWD is exempted from the
provisions of Section 84308 by the language of subdivision
(d), which exempts certain legislative codes.  You have
urged this result despite the fact that MWD does not meet the
requirements of Regulation 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18438.1
which specifies which bodies constitute legislative bodies
exempted by Section 84308(d).

     In support of your position you have cited the case of
Metropolitan Water Dist. v. Whitsett (1932) 215 Cal. 400,
418.  Having reviewed the case and the statutes on which it

_________________________
1/ All statutory references are to the Government Code
unless otherwise noted.

Jarlach Oley
April 19, 1983
Page 2


is based,2/ we cannot agree. The delegation to MWD by the
state legislature of the power to adopt ordinances,
resolutions and orders (Water Code Appendix Section 109-61)
does not make MWD a "legislative body" within the meaning of
Section 84308(d). MWD may well have certain quasi-
legislative powers, but these powers do not transform MWD's
board into a "legislative body" as that term is used in
Section 84308(d).3/

Nevertheless, unless MWD's board makes decisions on
applications for permits, licenses or other entitlements for
use, its activities would not be covered by section 84308.
Across-the-board rate-setting decisions, for example, do not
involve individual applications and are quasi-legislative.
Hence, they would not be covered.

With respect to MWD's annexation decisions, you should be
advised that the Commission has agreed to issue an opinion on
the application of Section 84308 to LAFCO's, which make
annexation decisions.


2/ The Whitsett case reviewed Water Code Appendix
Section 35-1, et seq., the Metropolitan Water District Act of
1927, which was repealed in 1969 and re-enacted at Water Code
Appendix Section 109-1, et seq.

3/ In enacting Section 84308 (AB 1040, Chap. 1049,
Stats. 1982) the legislature did not exempt all legislative
bodies. Instead, it chose to exempt only those legislative
bodies "such as city councils, county boards of supervisors,
and the State Legislature...." MWD does not fall within this
more narrow exemption. In adopting the regulations implementing Section 84308, the Commission specifically considered
and rejected an alternative wording of regulation 2 Cal. Adm.
Code Section 11438.1, which might have exempted MWD. It is
the Commission's interpretation of the legislature's intent,
in establishing the exemption, that only directly elected
legislative bodies such as city councils, etc., be exempt.
Absent that qualification, virtually all boards and
commissions are denominated as "legislative bodies" by some
statute somewhere. This is the result which the legislature
sought to avoid.

Jarlath Oley
April 19, 1983
Page 3


     Should you have further questions, please do not hesitate
to contact this office.  I may be reached at 916/322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Robert E. Leidigh
                                        Counsel
                                        Legal Division

REL:km

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California

Office of General Counsel

March 18, 1983


Mr. Ted Prim
Executive Director
Fair Political Practices Commission
P. O. Box 807
Sacramento, California  95804

Dear Mr. Prim:

     Fair Political Practices Commission
Bulletin dated February 7, 1983,
Article titled:  "New Campaign
Contribution Law to Alter Political
Landscape"

     We recently received our copy of the February 7, 1983
issue of the Fair Political Practices Commission bulletin.
We noted your article titled "New Campaign Contribution Law
to Alter Political Landscape" made reference to the Metropolitan
Water District as being one of the entities affected by the
law.  The law the article was referring to was Stats. 1982,
Ch. 1049, which added Section 84308 to the Political Reform
Act.

     For two separate and independent reasons, we do not
believe this law was intended to apply to the Metropolitan
Water District.  First, the District does not receive
applications for licenses, permits or entitlements for use
from private persons.  The District is composed of 27 member
public agencies.  All of the agencies are public entities.

     Water is wholesaled to these entities by the District
at uniform rates based upon the class of service involved.
Service connection requests are accepted only from the public
entities, as are annexation requests.  Our member public
agencies only request service connections when they determine
the connections are necessary to permit adequate water for
further wholesaling or for retailing.


1111 Sunset Boulevard, Los Angeles, Calif. / Mailing address: Box 54153, Los Angeles, Calif. 90054 / Telephone: (213) 250-6000

The Metropolitan Water District of Southern California


Mr. Ted Prim                                -2-                    March 18, 1983




            Territory is only annexed to the District when
      the territory is concurrently annexed to the member public
      agency or when it has been previously annexed to the agency.
      The District does not accept annexation requests from private
      parties.  Furthermore, once an annexation is accomplished,
      private parties have no resulting rights or entitlements
      to water.  The annexation merely permits the member public
      agency to request any supplemental water it may need to
      service the water demands of its jurisdiction.  Only private
      persons having need for water service can only obtain such
      service from the retailer, according to the retailer's
      rules and regulations.  Metropolitan does not retail water
      to any private persons.

            Secondly, we believe that subsection (d) of Government
      Code Section 84308 exempts the District's Board of Directors.
      It provides, in pertinent part, that:

            "Legislative bodies such as city councils,
            county boards of supervisors, and the State
            Legislature, while acting as a body or a
            committee thereof, are exempted from the
            coverage of [Section 84308]."
            (Emphasis added.)

            It is clear that the District's Board is a legislative body.
      (Metropolitan Water District v. Whitsett (1932) 215 Cal. 400, 418.)
      The Commission's own regulation at Section 18438.1(c), too,
      excludes legislative bodies from the definition of "quasi-judicial
      body or commission."  We note that Section 18438.1(a) purports
      to limit the definition of "legislative body" to elected bodies.
      We fail to see any distinction between legislative bodies which
      are elected and those which are appointed.  No such distinction
      is to be found in Section 84308(d).

            We therefore request that you reconsider the
      statement in the bulletin that the amendment affects the
      Metropolitan Water District.

                                          Very truly yours,

                                          Carl Boronkay
                                          General Counsel

                                          Jarlath Oley
                                          Deputy General Counsel

State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662          322-5660          322-5901          322-6441          322-6644

March 22, 1983


Jarlath Oley
Deputy General Counsel
Metropolitan Water District
of Southern California
Box 54153
Los Angeles, CA  90054

Re: A-83-074

Dear Mr. Oley:

Your letter requesting advice under the Political
Reform Act has been referred to Robert E. Leidigh, an
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political
Practices Commission. If you have any questions about your
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at
(916) 322-5901.

We try to answer all advice requests promptly.
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21
working days.

Very truly yours,

Barbara A. Milman
Chief, Legal Division

BAM:kn
Enclosure

sections.question: Does Government Code Section 84308, as amended by AB 1040 (Chapter 1049, Stats. 1982), apply to the Metropolitan Water District (MWD)? Is MWD exempted as a 'legislative body' under Section 84308(d)? Does MWD receive applications for permits, licenses, or entitlements for use from private parties such that Section 84308 would apply?
sections.conclusion: The Commission cannot agree that MWD is exempted from Section 84308(d) merely because it has quasi-legislative powers delegated by the state legislature. The MWD's board is not a 'legislative body' within the meaning of Section 84308(d)—only directly elected legislative bodies such as city councils, county boards of supervisors, and the State Legislature are exempted. However, unless MWD's board makes decisions on applications for permits, licenses, or other entitlements for use, its activities would not be covered by Section 84308. Across-the-board rate-setting decisions would not be covered as they are quasi-legislative in nature. The Commission further noted it would issue a separate opinion regarding the application of Section 84308 to LAFCOs regarding annexation decisions.
sections.question_synthetic: The question asks whether the Metropolitan Water District is covered by Government Code Section 84308's quasi-judicial decision-making requirements, specifically whether MWD qualifies as an exempted 'legislative body' under Section 84308(d) and whether it makes decisions on applications for permits, licenses, or entitlements that would trigger the statute's coverage.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concludes that MWD is not a 'legislative body' exempt from Section 84308 under the statute's narrow exemption for directly elected bodies like city councils. However, MWD would only be covered by Section 84308 if it makes decisions on individual applications for permits, licenses, or entitlements; rate-setting and quasi-legislative decisions would not trigger coverage.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document includes both the incoming request letter (from MWD dated March 18, 1983) and the FPPC response letter (dated April 19, 1983). The response clearly identifies the question and reasoning. Incoming letter articulates the statutory arguments MWD raised. High confidence in extraction of legal analysis and conclusion.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Does Government Code Section 84308, as amended by AB 1040 (Chapter 1049, Stats. 1982), apply to the Metropolitan Water District (MWD)? Is MWD exempted as a 'legislative body' under Section 84308(d)? Does MWD receive applications for permits, licenses, or entitlements for use from private parties such that Section 84308 would apply?

CONCLUSION: The Commission cannot agree that MWD is exempted from Section 84308(d) merely because it has quasi-legislative powers delegated by the state legislature. The MWD's board is not a 'legislative body' within the meaning of Section 84308(d)—only directly elected legislative bodies such as city councils, county boards of supervisors, and the State Legislature are exempted. However, unless MWD's board makes decisions on applications for permits, licenses, or other entitlements for use, its activities would not be covered by Section 84308. Across-the-board rate-setting decisions would not be covered as they are quasi-legislative in nature. The Commission further noted it would issue a separate opinion regarding the application of Section 84308 to LAFCOs regarding annexation decisions.

embedding.summary: This is an FPPC response to MWD's March 18, 1983 letter challenging an FPPC bulletin statement that Section 84308 (regarding quasi-judicial decision-making) applies to MWD. The FPPC rejects MWD's argument that it qualifies as an exempted 'legislative body,' but notes that Section 84308 would only apply to MWD if it makes decisions on individual applications for permits, licenses, or entitlements.
